examples.title: Basic File Writing and Reading
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a file with ofstream, writing data, then reading it back with ifstream with proper error checking
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates basic file I/O. It creates 'sample.txt' using ofstream (output file stream), writes three lines of formatted data, closes the file, then reopens it with ifstream (input file stream) to read and display the contents. It also shows proper error checking with the ! operator and checking eof()/fail() after reading.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of characters

examples.title: Binary File Operations with Random Access
examples.description: Shows binary file writing, seeking to specific positions, and reading using fstream with random access capabilities
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates binary file I/O with random access. It defines a Student structure and writes three records to a binary file using fstream with ios::binary. It then uses seekg() to randomly position the read pointer to access specific records without reading sequentially. The reinterpret_cast is used to treat the struct memory as char array for binary write/read operations.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for seek operations, O(n) where n is record size for read/write

examples.title: Formatted and Unformatted I/O Comparison
examples.description: Demonstrates the difference between formatted I/O (<<, >>) and unformatted I/O (get, getline, read, put, write) with practical examples
examples.explanation: This program contrasts formatted and unformatted I/O. Formatted I/O uses << and >> operators which handle type conversion automatically (int to string, etc.). Unformatted I/O operates on raw characters: get() reads character-by-character including whitespace, getline() reads until delimiter, put() writes single character, write() writes raw byte blocks. Formatted is easier for human-readable data; unformatted gives precise control and is required for binary data.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of characters processed
